concept: The most common opening question in IB interviews. Interviewers want to see that you understand all three statements and how they connect.
question: Walk me through the three financial statements.
correctAnswer: The income statement shows revenue minus expenses to arrive at net income — it measures profitability over a period. The balance sheet shows what a company owns (assets) and owes (liabilities), with the difference being equity — it's a snapshot in time. The cash flow statement shows actual cash movement: starting with net income, adjusting for non-cash items, and showing cash from operations, investing, and financing. Net income links all three: it flows to retained earnings on the balance sheet and is the starting point of the cash flow statement.

concept: Profit is an accounting concept. Cash is real money. They differ because of timing: revenue can be earned before cash arrives, and expenses can be recorded before they're paid.
question: Why can a profitable company still run out of cash?
correctAnswer: Profit is an accounting measure — revenue is recorded when earned, not when cash is received. A company could show a profit while waiting 60-90 days to collect from customers (accounts receivable). Meanwhile, it still has to pay suppliers and employees. That's why companies can be profitable and cash-poor at the same time. Cash flow is a separate, equally important metric.
